narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on their academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 28.50, Year 1 school decile of 5th, Year 3 decile of 4th, middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 52.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 1 school decile ranking. Your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, as it stands notably below the typical applicant's average of around 37, signaling to the model a higher risk despite your other strengths. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA worked strongly in your favor, exceeding the dataset average of 3.21 and pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 5—slightly below the average of 5.71—provided a moderate positive influence, suggesting solid early performance relative to peers that helped offset some concerns. Compared to typical students, your full-time status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%), and your middle-income background is common, so they played smaller roles here. While these top factors paint a mixed picture, the LSAT's dominant negative impact outweighed the boosts from your GPA and early grades. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, as even a modest improvement could shift your predicted odds significantly. Strengthening your overall academic narrative through additional coursework or recommendations might also help in reapplying. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting that key weakness—your determination as an Asian male applicant positions you well to overcome this hurdle.
